violations.detail: Cards 1 and 3 test the same stack-vs-heap speed comparison from opposite angles. 'Why is stack allocation faster?' and 'Why is heap allocation slower?' are the same fact from two sides — a sacrifice/gain mirror pair per SKILL.md. Keep one or merge.

violations.rule: Back brevity — semicolon joins two independent facts
violations.detail: Back reads 'The heap supports runtime-sized allocations; the stack requires sizes known at compile time'. Semicolon joins two independent clauses, each a separate fact. SKILL.md explicitly lists 'A; B → semicolon joins two independent facts' as a violation pattern.

violations.rule: Back brevity — comma + qualifier adds second fact
violations.detail: Back reads 'Exceeding the fixed stack size, usually from deep recursion'. The comma + 'usually from' appends the most common cause as a second fact beyond the direct answer. Should split into two cards or trim.

violations.rule: Back brevity — parenthetical adds new fact (not abbreviation)
violations.detail: Back reads 'The programmer (manually, via free/delete)'. The parenthetical adds HOW (manually) and specific functions (free/delete), which is new information — not a standard abbreviation. SKILL.md allows abbreviation parentheticals but not new-fact parentheticals.

Cloze '{{c1::returns}}' is guessable from surrounding context. 'Stack variables are automatically deallocated when the function ___' has only one plausible completion — 'returns' is trivially deducible from the words 'function' and 'automatically deallocated'.

violations.rule: Specific constant value without understanding why
violations.detail: Card asks for the typical stack size limit and answers '1-8 MB'. This is a specific numeric value that does not test understanding of why that range matters or what consequences it has. Card selection guide explicitly lists 'specific constant values without understanding why they matter' as not worth cards.
